html:
A 3D line projects to an image line
⚠ Figure failed to load

elements: camera center (origin) represented as a small sphere
semi-transparent vertical image plane at Z = f with a very small, unobtrusive label 'Image Plane (Z = f)' positioned at its bottom corner
a continuous 3D line extending from P_0 into the background along direction D
a sequence of four 3D points on the line: P_t, P_{t+1}, P_{t+2}, and P_{t+3}, rendered as colored spheres along the 3D line
corresponding projected points p_t, p_{t+1}, p_{t+2}, and p_{t+3} on the image plane, rendered as smaller spheres with dynamic vertical/horizontal label offsets to prevent overlaps
four dashed projection rays connecting the camera center to each of the four 3D points, passing through their respective projections on the image plane
the projected 2D line segment on the image plane, showing the path of the projections converging toward the vanishing point
the vanishing point v on the image plane, highlighted with a distinct bright indicator
a parallel reference ray (dashed line) starting from the camera center and pointing in direction D, piercing the image plane exactly at the vanishing point v
a direction vector D visualization (arrow) positioned near the start of the 3D line

interactions.label: Base Parameter t
interactions.teaches: Shifts the sequence of 3D points farther down the line, showing how their projections bunch up closer and closer to the vanishing point as they recede

interactions.label: Line Yaw (Direction)
interactions.teaches: Rotating the 3D line's direction vector shifts the parallel ray, demonstrating that the vanishing point is entirely determined by orientation, not position

interactions.label: Focal Length f
interactions.teaches: Varying the focal length moves the image plane physically closer or farther, scaling the position of the vanishing point and projections proportionally from the center

interactions.label: Show Parallel Ray
interactions.teaches: Toggles the ray from the camera center parallel to the line, geometrically proving why the vanishing point represents the line at infinity

demo_steps.title: Projecting sequence of 3D points
demo_steps.narration: A camera at the origin observes a 3D line. Look at the sequence of four points (P_t to P_t+3) along the line. Each point projects a ray back to the camera center, intersecting the semi-transparent image plane on the left. Even though the 3D points are evenly spaced, notice how their 2D projections (p_t to p_t+3) begin to group closer together.

demo_steps.title: Receding to infinity
demo_steps.narration: As we increase the base parameter t, the entire sequence of 3D points slides further into the background. Watch the 2D projected points on the image plane: they compress dramatically, converging towards a single terminal point. This compression is the direct physical consequence of the perspective perspective 1/Z scaling.

demo_steps.title: Defining the vanishing point
demo_steps.narration: Let's overlay the parallel ray starting from the camera center. Since this ray is perfectly parallel to the 3D line, they only intersect at infinity. The projection of this intersection at infinity is the vanishing point (v), located exactly where this parallel ray pierces the image plane. It acts as the absolute boundary for the projected line.

demo_steps.title: Changing the line's orientation
demo_steps.narration: Rotate the 3D line. The parallel ray and the 3D line rotate in unison. As a result, the vanishing point shifts across the image plane. This highlights a fundamental rule of projective geometry: parallel lines in 3D space share the exact same direction vector, and thus always converge to the same vanishing point.

demo_steps.title: Scaling with focal length
demo_steps.narration: Finally, we increase the camera's focal length. The physical image plane shifts farther from the camera center. This magnifies the entire projected image, pushing the vanishing point and the point projections outward from the center, illustrating how camera zoom alters the scale of perspective projection.

plan.camera_suggestion: Perspective camera positioned at X = -5, Y = 3.5, Z = -1, target set to X = 1.5, Y = 0.5, Z = 6. This mimics the textbook sketch by placing the projection center and the image plane clearly on the left-hand side, while the 3D line and its points recede naturally into the right-hand background.
plan.notes: To ensure readability and prevent overlap issues identified in the feedback, render the 'image plane Z = f' label as a tiny text sprite anchored strictly to the bottom-left corner of the image plane. For the point labels, compute dynamic offsets: offset the 3D point labels (P_t, etc.) slightly upward in Y, and offset the projected point labels (p_t, etc.) horizontally away from the projected line segment. The vanishing point v must be updated reactively as v = (f * Dx / Dz, f * Dy / Dz, f).

plan.contextChunk:
plane. Let's start writing the parametric vector form of a 3D line using
heterogeneous coordinates. We can parameterize a 3D line with a point,
$\mathbf{P}_0=[X_0, Y_0, Z_0]^\mathsf{T}$, and a direction vector,
$\mathbf{D} = [D_X, D_Y, D_Z]^\mathsf{T}$, as:

$$\mathbf{P}_t = 
    \begin{bmatrix}
    X_0 + t D_x\\
    Y_0 + t D_y\\
    Z_0 + t D_z
    \end{bmatrix}
$${#eq-parametric_vector_line} The 3D point $\mathbf{P}_t$ moves along a
line when the independent variable $t$ goes from $-\infty$ to $\infty$.
A camera located in the world coordinates origin will observe a 2D line
resulting from projecting the 3D line into the camera plane as shown in
the sketch shown in @fig-vanishing_points.

![Projection of a 3D line onto the camera plane. The 3D line and the camera center form a plane (the camera center is a the world-coordinates origin). The intersection of this plane with the camera plane is the 2D line that appears in the image. The image point $\mathbf{v](figures/single_view_3d/vanishing_points.png){width="100%" #fig-vanishing_points}

We can get the equation of the 2D line that appears in the image by
using the perspective projection equations, resulting in:
$$\mathbf{p}_t = 
    \begin{bmatrix}
    x_t\\
    y_t
    \end{bmatrix}
    =
    \begin{bmatrix}
    f \dfrac{ X_0 + t D_X}{Z_0 + t D_Z}\\[12pt]
    f \dfrac{ Y_0 + t D_Y}{Z_0 + t D_Z}
    \end{bmatrix}
$${#eq-line_projection}


[...]

$\boldsymbol{l}_4$, we can get another vanishing point by computing
their cross-product. Finally, the horizon line is the line that passes
through both points that can be computed as the cross-product of the
vanishing points in homogeneous coordinates.

![Using parallel lines, in homogeneous coordinates, to compute vanishing points and the horizon line.](figures/imaging_geometry/horizon_line.png){width="90%" #fig-horizon_line_cross_products}

### Detecting Vanishing Points

@fig-vanishing_lines shows the office scene and three vanishing points
computed manually. In this scene there are three dominant orientations
along orthogonal directions. The three vanishing points are estimated by
selecting pairs of long edges in the image corresponding to parallel 3D
lines in the scene and finding their intersection.

![Most object boundaries in this photograph are oriented parallel to one of the world-coordinates reference frame (the world-coordinates frame is chosen in this way on purpose). All the parallel 3D lines converge on the image plane in a vanishing point. In this picture there are three main vanishing points, each of them related to one of the world-coordinates frame.](figures/single_view_3d/office_vanishing_points_3.png){width="100%" #fig-vanishing_lines}

Vanishing points can be extracted automatically from images by first
detecting image edges and their orientations (like we did in chapter
@sec-simplesystem) and then using RANSAC (@sec-RANSAC) to group the edges: every pair of edges will
vote for a vanishing point and the point with the largest support will
be considered a vanishing point.

There are also learning-based approaches for vanishing point detection
using graphical models @coughlan1999manhattan, or deep learning based
approaches such as DeepVP @Chang2018DeepVPDL and NeurVPS
@zhou2019neurvps.

## Measuring Heights Using Parallel Lines

Let's now use what we have seen up to now to measurement distances in

elements: camera center (origin) with coordinate axes X, Y, Z
semi-transparent image plane at Z = f
3D line in space represented as a continuous red tube/line
direction vector D plotted as a black arrow parallel to the 3D line, starting at the origin
direction vector D plotted as an arrow lying directly on the 3D line
moving 3D point P_t on the line, labeled
projection ray (dashed grey line) from the origin to P_t
projected point p_t on the image plane, labeled
projected 2D line on the image plane (red segment)
vanishing point v (bright yellow dot) on the image plane where the parallel ray from the origin intersects it
parallel projection ray (dashed black line) from the origin through the vanishing point v towards infinity

interactions.label: Parameter t
interactions.teaches: Moving the point P_t along the 3D line demonstrates how its 2D projection p_t approaches the vanishing point v as t goes to infinity

interactions.label: Line Yaw (Direction)
interactions.teaches: Rotating the line's direction vector D shifts the position of the vanishing point on the image plane, showing that the vanishing point depends purely on line orientation

interactions.label: Focal Length f
interactions.teaches: Changing the focal length scales the image plane projection, moving the vanishing point outward or inward from the principal point (optical center)

interactions.label: Show Parallel Ray
interactions.teaches: Overlays the ray starting from the camera center parallel to the line's direction, geometrically defining the vanishing point

demo_steps.title: Projecting a 3D line
demo_steps.narration: A camera at the origin observes a 3D line parameterized by P_t = P_0 + t*D. At t = 1.0, the point P_t projects to p_t on the image plane (at Z = f). The projection is determined by where the visual ray (dashed line) intersects the image plane.

demo_steps.title: Receding to infinity
demo_steps.narration: Watch what happens as we increase t to 10, pushing P_t deep into the scene. Its projected coordinate p_t moves along the red path on the image plane, but its speed decelerates dramatically, converging towards a limiting spot on the canvas.

demo_steps.title: The vanishing point
demo_steps.narration: Now we reveal the parallel ray starting from the camera center (the origin) in direction D. Since the ray is parallel to the 3D line, they intersect only at infinity. The projection of this intersection point is the vanishing point v (yellow circle), which lies exactly where this parallel ray pierces the image plane.

demo_steps.title: Changing the line direction
demo_steps.narration: Let's change the orientation (Yaw) of the 3D line. As the line rotates, both the line and the parallel ray rotate in unison. Notice how the vanishing point v moves across the image plane. This demonstrates that the vanishing point is independent of the line's starting point and is determined purely by its 3D direction vector D.

demo_steps.title: Zooming with focal length
demo_steps.narration: Finally, let's increase the focal length f. This pushes the image plane further from the projection center, scaling the coordinate of the vanishing point outward. This acts like an optical zoom, magnifying the spacing of the projected lines while the physical 3D geometry remains unchanged.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Perspective camera positioned at X=-3, Y=2, Z=1, looking towards the midpoint of the image plane and the 3D line (around X=1, Y=0.5, Z=4) to give a clear side-isometric depth perspective of both the projection plane and the receding line.
interactionPlan.notes: Ensure that the vanishing point v on the image plane is computed dynamically as v = (f * Dx / Dz, f * Dy / Dz, f). The projected point p_t must be computed similarly using the coordinates of P_t. Use distinct colors: red for the 3D line and its 2D projection, black for the direction vectors and parallel ray, and yellow for the vanishing point.

attempts.html:
Projecting a 3D line
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The label 'image plane Z = f' is rendered at a massive size, completely obscuring the 3D elements.

The original figure displays a sequence of multiple points (P_t-1, P_t, P_t+1, P_t+2) and their projections simultaneously to illustrate how they converge to the vanishing point; the generated version only shows one point at a time based on a slider.
The camera angle is poorly positioned, making the 3D elements look compressed and disorganized compared to the clear, expanded layout of the source diagram.
evaluation.notes: The concept is mathematically implemented, but the visual layout is ruined by an oversized label blocking the screen and an awkward camera angle.
action_items: Significantly reduce the font size of the 'image plane Z = f' label and position it so it does not obscure the main elements.
Adjust the camera position and target to mimic the textbook's clean perspective, where the image plane is on the left and the 3D line extends clearly into the background on the right.
Render the full sequence of points P_t-1, P_t, P_t+1, P_t+2 and their projections simultaneously to match the original figure's visual explanation of vanishing point convergence.
Ensure labels like Pt, pt, and D do not overlap each other when camera parameters change.

feedback.rationale: The plan failed to capture the core conceptual element of showing a sequence of multiple points simultaneously to illustrate convergence, opting instead for a single-point slider which ruins the educational value of the diagram.
action_items: Significantly reduce the font size of the 'image plane Z = f' label and position it so it does not obscure the main elements.
Adjust the camera position and target to mimic the textbook's clean perspective, where the image plane is on the left and the 3D line extends clearly into the background on the right.
Render the full sequence of points P_t-1, P_t, P_t+1, P_t+2 and their projections simultaneously to match the original figure's visual explanation of vanishing point convergence.
Ensure labels like Pt, pt, and D do not overlap each other when camera parameters change.

Ensure labels like Pt, pt, and D do not overlap each other when camera parameters change.

attempts.html:
A 3D line and its image
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The camera center (origin with green axes X, Y, Z) is on the right side looking left, whereas the original has it on the left looking right.
The 3D line is not red in the generated figure (it appears black/cyan), whereas the original shows a prominent red 3D line parallel to the vanishing ray.
The parallel vector arrows 'D' are misaligned and do not clearly show the parallel relationship between the line direction and the vanishing point ray.
Severe text and label overlap (e.g., 'D', 'P_t+2', 'P_t+1' are clumped together on the left).
The layout of the projected points on the image plane vs 3D space is cramped and distorted compared to the clean, spacious original.
evaluation.notes: The 3D scene is set up backwards (camera looking from right to left) and suffers from severe label overlapping and incorrect coloring of the 3D line.
action_items: Flip the setup so the camera center (origin with green X, Y, Z axes) is on the left, looking right through the image plane.
Color the 3D line red to match the original figure, ensuring it is a single continuous straight line.
Align the two 'D' vector arrows so they are perfectly parallel to the 3D line and the vanishing ray.
Increase the spacing/scale of the 3D scene elements to prevent labels like 'D', 'P_t', and 'p_t' from overlapping.
Adjust the initial camera angle to frame the scene from a clear, slightly elevated front-left perspective.

feedback.rationale: The overall concept is correct, but the execution has several geometric, layout, and visual bugs (such as a reversed camera setup, incorrect colors, and severe label overlap) that can be resolved by refining the generator implementation.
action_items: Flip the setup so the camera center (origin with green X, Y, Z axes) is on the left, looking right through the image plane.

Increase the spacing/scale of the 3D scene elements to prevent labels like 'D', 'P_t', and 'p_t' from overlapping.
Adjust the initial camera angle to frame the scene from a clear, slightly elevated front-left perspective.

attempts.html:
A 3D line projects to an image line
⚠ Figure failed to load

discrepancies: The segment of the 3D line between P_t and P_{t+1} is rendered in red, whereas in the source figure it is a prominent solid black arrow/vector labelled 'D'.
The parallel black vector 'D' extending from the vanishing point v is misaligned/separated by a gap from the dashed projection ray.
The label for p_{t+2} on the image plane is missing the overline/bar present in the source figure (which shows \bar{p}_{t+2}).
An extra black arrow helper and label 'D' are drawn near the coordinate origin, which do not appear in the original figure.
evaluation.notes: Excellent and highly interactive implementation of projective geometry, with minor differences in vector representation and line segmentation color compared to the source.
action_items: Render the 3D line segment between P_t and P_{t+1} as a solid black arrow/vector labelled 'D' to match the original layout.
Align the black vector 'D' near the top right so that it starts exactly from the vanishing point 'v' as a continuous extension of the dashed line.
Change the HTML label for p_{t+2} to pt+2 to correctly represent \bar{p}_{t+2}.
Remove the duplicate black arrow 'D' starting directly from the origin to simplify and match the reference illustration.

feedback.rationale: The conceptual model and interactivity are outstanding (scores of 5), but minor visual bugs, incorrect primitives/colors, and small label formatting errors require generator-level refinements.

Change the HTML label for p_{t+2} to pt+2 to correctly represent \bar{p}_{t+2}.
Remove the duplicate black arrow 'D' starting directly from the origin to simplify and match the reference illustration.
